text.title: anyhow for Applications and Combinators
text.summary: anyhow for application-level error handling, context and bail macros, Result/Option combinators, and async error handling.

rule.statement: Use anyhow for application binary code where callers do not need to match on specific error variants. Use typed errors (thiserror) for library code where callers must be able to distinguish errors.
rule.rationale: Libraries have callers who need to handle errors programmatically. Applications catch errors at the top level and display them. anyhow's dynamic error type is ideal for applications, typed enums for libraries.

example.note: context() adds a human-readable message to any error. bail! returns an Err early with a new error message.

list.item: result.map(|v| transform(v)) — transform the Ok value
result.map_err(|e| convert(e)) — transform the Err value
result.and_then(|v| fallible_op(v)) — chain a fallible operation on Ok
result.or_else(|_| fallback()) — provide a fallback on Err
result.unwrap_or(default) — extract Ok or return default on Err
result.unwrap_or_else(|_| compute()) — lazy default on Err

rule.statement: In async functions, ? propagates errors across await points. Use futures::future::join_all() to run multiple async operations concurrently; call .into_iter().collect::<Result<Vec<_>>>() to fail on first error.
rule.rationale: join_all returns Vec<Result<T>>. Collecting into a single Result<Vec> is the idiomatic way to propagate the first failure while preserving error context.
